summary: Gruber responds to criticism that his dismissal of Satya Nadella's jargon-laden layoff memo could equally apply to Apple's IBM partnership press release. He concedes the point partially—the Apple press release is indeed opaque—but argues the real comparison should be between memos of the same type: Tim Cook's internal company-wide memo versus Nadella's. Cook's memo is short, clear, and jargon-free, while Nadella's raises more questions than it answers. Gruber concludes that clear writing reflects clear thinking, and the difference reveals deeper cultural gaps between the two companies.
key_points: Gruber acknowledges a fair critique that Apple's IBM press release was also jargon-laden, but attributes this to it being jointly written with IBM
The proper comparison is memo-to-memo, not press release-to-memo—and Cook's internal memo is markedly clearer than Nadella's
Clear writing is presented as evidence of clear thinking, not merely a stylistic preference
The contrast between Cook's and Nadella's communication styles reflects fundamentally different company cultures at Apple and Microsoft
money_quotes: I think clear writing is the result of clear thinking.
Cook's memo isn't casual or informal; it simply isn't dressed up with extraneous formalities and corporate-culture bromides.
To compare a press release to a company-wide memo is a bit of an apple-to-oranges situation.
Nadella's raises as many questions as it answers.
My only criticism is that Cook doesn't use the Oxford comma, but that's a matter of taste.
key_insight: Clear writing reflects clear thinking, and the contrast between Tim Cook's plain-spoken memo and Satya Nadella's jargon-heavy one reveals a fundamental cultural difference between Apple and Microsoft.
